metadata.purpose: Aureoboletus, Hemileccinum, Xerocomus-like, and related textured-cap boletes.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Old Man of the Woods is kept under the common-name slug. The former scientific-name slug strobilomyces-strobilaceus has been merged here to avoid duplicate listings.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Usually skipped. If used by experienced foragers, only young firm pieces are worth considering.
records.edibility_detail: Not a prime edible. Use only young firm specimens if at all; older specimens are poor textured. Treat as a novelty/caution bolete, not a beginner meal.
records.general_notes: Hard to confuse with choice boletes because of the shaggy black-gray cap and blackening flesh. Species-level Strobilomyces taxonomy can be fiddly.
records.field_identification: Distinctive gray to black shaggy/scaly bolete with woolly cap and stem. Pores start whitish gray and darken; cut flesh often reddens or pinks, then darkens toward black. Overall look is sooty and ragged.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Old Man of the Woods
use_links.notes: Field identification and comparison source; ID/taxonomy only, not edible-use clearance.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Strobilomyces overview
use_links.notes: Strobilomyces group comparison; ID/taxonomy only, not edible-use clearance.

use_links.label: Missouri Department of Conservation: Old Man of the Woods
use_links.notes: Supports field identification, mixed-hardwood habitat, lookalike caution, and edible-use framing: edible when young/fresh, with older specimens becoming poor-quality/tasteless; also supports small-first-serving caution.

texture: Firm when young; tough or spongy when old

records.edibility_notes: Edible by some accounts when young, but usually poor quality; included mainly because it is distinctive and useful for ID practice. Not a prime edible.

edible_use.notes: Not a prime edible. Use only young firm specimens if at all; older specimens are poor textured. Treat as a novelty/caution bolete, not a beginner meal.

records.duplicate_note: Duplicate Old Man of the Woods record kept only as a legacy slug alias.

records.mushroom_card_note: Sooty shaggy bolete; edible only when young and firm, but usually mediocre food. MDC supports young/fresh edible-use framing; retain species-complex caution.
records.caution_reason: Young firm specimens only; poor food quality; not a beginner meal.
source_review_notes: MDC agency field guide supports the cautious edible-use framing: edible when young/fresh, with older specimens becoming poor-quality/tasteless; it also supports first-time edible-mushroom caution.
Keep MushroomExpert links for ID/taxonomy and retain Strobilomyces species-complex caution; do not treat this as a clean beginner edible.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds a limited group-level spore-print description and normalized filters; finer separation remains review-sensitive and may require a thick print or microscopy.

records.culinary_uses: Reported edible, but keep as a niche species unless you know it well.
records.notes: Russell's Bolete: yellow-brown to reddish-brown cracked cap and a shaggy/jagged-looking stem; edible reports but not a top-tier target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Russell's Bolete: yellow-brown to reddish-brown cracked cap and a shaggy/jagged-looking stem; edible reports but not a top-tier target.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; treated as a summer-and-fall eastern bolete until local timing is better documented.
research_notes: Still blocked from normal cards: species-level source added, but no exact public-usable image is verified.
Look for the shaggy or jagged stem surface, yellow-brown to reddish-brown cap that may crack into patches, and oak/hemlock/pine associations.
Suppressed from normal cards because no exact public-usable image is verified.

records.mushroom_card_note: Russell's Bolete: yellow-brown to reddish-brown cracked cap and a shaggy/jagged-looking stem; edible reports but not a top-tier target.
records.field_identification: Look for the shaggy or jagged stem surface, yellow-brown to reddish-brown cap that may crack into patches, and oak/hemlock/pine associations.
records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.
records.food_value: Not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: Current confidence, food value, or field separation is not strong enough for a food recommendation. Keep searchable for comparison and field ID.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.
records.image_coverage_note: Exact public-usable image not verified; do not use placeholder art or generic relatives as a species image.
records.suppression_reason: Missing exact public-usable species image; suppressed from normal cards until reviewed image support exists.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Aureoboletus russellii
use_links.notes: Species-level source replacing prior Wikipedia-only source; record stays hidden until exact public-usable image exists.

records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Occasional edible when young and firm; not a prime beginner bolete.

staining: Usually not staining; occasionally weak blue bruising on pores

records.edibility_detail: Use only with confident pine-associated long-stemmed bolete ID. Confirm the very long ridged stem, yellow pores, prominent white basal mycelium, and absence of dangerous red-pored traits.
records.field_identification: Mycorrhizal with pines. Look for a reddish-brown to brown cap, yellow pore surface, very long ridged to nearly reticulate stem, prominent white basal mycelium, and a cap margin that can project beyond the pore surface.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Aureoboletus projectellus
use_links.notes: Pine association, field marks, season, and staining notes.

records.edibility_notes: Use only with confident pine-associated long-stemmed bolete ID. Confirm the very long ridged stem, yellow pores, prominent white basal mycelium, and absence of dangerous red-pored traits.

edible_use.notes: Use only with confident pine-associated long-stemmed bolete ID. Confirm the very long ridged stem, yellow pores, prominent white basal mycelium, and absence of dangerous red-pored traits.

lookalike_risk.label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Edible bolete use needs pore color, staining, host/substrate, taste where appropriate, and separation from false king, bitter Tylopilus, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining caution boletes.
required_checks: Reject bitter taste where safe nibble-and-spit is appropriate
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat unidentified boletes; red/orange pores, bitter taste, or violent blue staining mean stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.source_note: Third support image rights metadata normalized in v4.3.155; first two images were already rights-clean. v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Not collected for meals in this guide. Keep as an identification record until local use and species limits are checked.

mushroom_profile.summary: Oak-associated eastern bolete with a dry granular-young cap, white pores becoming yellow and sometimes bruising cinnamon, and a yellow stem with an orange apex and longitudinal lines.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; species-level local timing needs more checking.
cap_surface: Dry cap; granular when young, becoming smoother with age

records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Look for a dry cap that is granular when young and smoother later, white pores becoming yellow and sometimes bruising cinnamon, nonstaining flesh, and a yellow longitudinally lined stem with an orange apical zone under oak.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert Boletus key: Aureoboletus roxanae
use_links.notes: Hardwood association, yellow/greenish pores, dotted stem, and non-bruising pore surface.

records.edibility_notes: Yellow-pored, dotted-stem Aureoboletus/Xerocomus-type bolete. Do not infer kitchen use from nearby bolete relatives.

records.image_rights_notes: Image rights normalized in v4.3.158-r2026-06-18-commons-image-rights-cleanup1. Retained visible images have exact Commons file-page author, source, license, and reuse metadata.

records.taxonomy_note: The scientific name is retained, but v4.3.211 replaces profile traits that had been copied from neighboring Hemileccinum subglabripes.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food target; useful mainly as an identification species among cracked-cap and pitted/ridged-spored boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Odd cracked-cap bolete with a brown cap, yellow pores that slowly blue, scaber-like stem texture, and a habit of appearing near decayed oak wood.
research_notes: Check the brown to dark-brown cap that may crack, yellow pores that slowly stain blue then brown, and a stem with brown scaber-like dots or hairy/scaly patches. Often found near or from well-decayed oak wood, which is unusual for a bolete.

records.edibility_detail: Treat as an ID/comparison species rather than a reliable table mushroom.
records.mushroom_card_note: Odd cracked-cap bolete with a brown cap, yellow pores that slowly blue, scaber-like stem texture, and a habit of appearing near decayed oak wood.
records.field_identification: Check the brown to dark-brown cap that may crack, yellow pores that slowly stain blue then brown, and a stem with brown scaber-like dots or hairy/scaly patches. Often found near or from well-decayed oak wood, which is unusual for a bolete.

records.edibility_notes: Treat as an ID/comparison species rather than a reliable table mushroom.

records.culinary_uses: Not a practical food target; included for recognition and comparison among red cracked-cap boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Reddish cracked-cap bolete with yellow pores that bruise blue and red-toned flesh in the stem; described from Michigan, but still a specialist ID species.
research_notes: Look for a brick-red to reddish cracked cap with pale flesh showing in the cracks, yellow pores that bruise blue then brown, and flesh that is yellow in the cap but reddish in the stem. Microscopic ridged spores separate it from look-alikes.

records.edibility_detail: Treat as a specialist ID species, not a beginner edible.
records.mushroom_card_note: Reddish cracked-cap bolete with yellow pores that bruise blue and red-toned flesh in the stem; described from Michigan, but still a specialist ID species.
records.field_identification: Look for a brick-red to reddish cracked cap with pale flesh showing in the cracks, yellow pores that bruise blue then brown, and flesh that is yellow in the cap but reddish in the stem. Microscopic ridged spores separate it from look-alikes.

records.culinary_uses: Generally treated as edible but not usually a top-tier table mushroom; larvae often ruin mature stems.

mushroom_profile.summary: Pretty and distinctive bolete with pinkish cap, pink scabers on the stem, and a chrome-yellow stem base.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Pinkish brown to cinnamon or purplish brown.
research_notes: The key combination is pinkish to tan cap, creamy pores becoming pinkish/brownish, pinkish scabers on the stem, and a bright chrome-yellow stem base/basal mycelium. Often formerly listed as Tylopilus or Leccinum chromapes.

records.edibility_detail: Edible with correct ID and fresh condition, but not prime.
records.mushroom_card_note: Pretty and distinctive bolete with pinkish cap, pink scabers on the stem, and a chrome-yellow stem base.
records.field_identification: The key combination is pinkish to tan cap, creamy pores becoming pinkish/brownish, pinkish scabers on the stem, and a bright chrome-yellow stem base/basal mycelium. Often formerly listed as Tylopilus or Leccinum chromapes.

records.culinary_uses: ID/comparison record only in this guide; not presented as a food target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Clustered brown bolete with a dry brown cap, bright yellow non-bruising pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a stem often swollen above a short root; treated here as an ID/comparison record.
research_notes: Look for clustered growth, dry brown to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow pores that do not bruise blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a stem swollen near the base before tapering into a short root.
MushroomExpert supports the Boletus innixus concept but treats the Aureoboletus placement cautiously; keep the app record taxonomy-cautious and non-food-forward.

records.general_notes: Image coverage is currently suppressed pending exact rights verification. MushroomExpert supports the Boletus innixus concept; the Aureoboletus placement should be treated as taxonomy-caution rather than food-use support.
records.edibility_detail: Use as an ID/comparison species only. MushroomExpert supports the Boletus innixus concept but does not provide edible-use support; do not present as a normal food target.
records.mushroom_card_note: Clustered brown bolete with a dry brown cap, bright yellow non-bruising pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a stem often swollen above a short root.
records.field_identification: Look for clustered growth, dry brown to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow pores that do not bruise blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a stem swollen near the base before tapering into a short root.

edible_use.notes: ID/comparison record only; no strong edible-use source is present in the active record.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus innixus
use_links.notes: Supports the Boletus innixus concept and provides taxonomy caution around the Aureoboletus placement; does not support edible-use promotion.

records.culinary_uses: Not a food target in this guide; retained for ID/comparison with shaggy Strobilomyces and dark oddball boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Shaggy Strobilomyces species close to Old Man of the Woods; North American taxonomy is unsettled and spores may be needed for reliable separation.
research_notes: Small erect blackish cap scales over pale gray ground, whitish pores that bruise reddish gray then black, and flesh that turns red then black.
Separating it from S. floccopus / Old Man of the Woods and other North American Strobilomyces can require spore characters; keep this record review-needed as intentional taxonomy/ID caution.
pore_color: Whitish pores bruising reddish then black

records.edibility_detail: Use as an ID/comparison species only in this guide. North American Strobilomyces taxonomy is unsettled, and field separation from similar shaggy Strobilomyces species may require spore characters; do not collect as a food target.
records.mushroom_card_note: Intentional ID/comparison record. North American Strobilomyces taxonomy is unsettled, and separation from similar shaggy Strobilomyces may require spore morphology.
records.field_identification: Small erect blackish cap scales over pale gray ground, whitish pores that bruise reddish gray then black, and flesh that turns red then black. Separation from S. floccopus / Old Man of the Woods and other North American Strobilomyces may require spore morphology.

records.culinary_uses: ID/comparison record only in this guide; poor/niche table value and not a normal food target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Slender hemlock-associated bolete with whitish pores that become pinkish, a long graceful stem, and a Tylopilus-like look.
staining: Variable; generally not a strong blue-stainer
research_notes: Long slender stem, whitish pore surface becoming pinkish to pinkish-brown, and association with hemlock or other conifers. Older guides may call it Tylopilus gracilis.

records.edibility_detail: Use as an ID/comparison species only. Confirm the long slender stem, whitish pores becoming pinkish, and hemlock/conifer association; do not present as a normal edible target.
records.mushroom_card_note: Slender hemlock-associated bolete with whitish pores that become pinkish, a long graceful stem, and a Tylopilus-like look.
records.field_identification: Long slender stem, whitish pore surface becoming pinkish to pinkish-brown, and association with hemlock or other conifers. Older guides may call it Tylopilus gracilis.
records.edibility_notes: Use as an ID/comparison species only. Confirm the long slender stem, whitish pores becoming pinkish, and hemlock/conifer association; do not present as a normal edible target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small sticky yellow-orange to reddish bolete with an unusually long, deeply reticulate stem; reported from Michigan but more southern/Appalachian in emphasis.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall mixed hardwood/pine-oak bolete.

records.mushroom_card_note: Small sticky yellow-orange to reddish bolete with an unusually long, deeply reticulate stem; reported from Michigan but more southern/Appalachian in emphasis.
records.field_identification: Check for sticky bright yellow/orange to reddish cap, yellow non-bruising pores, and a striking long stem that is deeply ridged or lacerate-reticulate. Oak or mixed oak-pine association.
records.edibility_detail: Treat as ID/comparison until local confidence is stronger. Confirm sticky yellow/orange to reddish cap, yellow non-bruising pores, long deeply reticulate stem, and oak or mixed oak-pine setting.
records.edibility_notes: Treat as ID/comparison until local confidence is stronger. Confirm sticky yellow/orange to reddish cap, yellow non-bruising pores, long deeply reticulate stem, and oak or mixed oak-pine setting.
records.source_note: v4.3.211 adds an exact-species or defensible complex-level macroscopic spore-print description and normalized filter values; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Occasional/niche edible comparison species; not a main target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Eastern oak-forest bolete with sticky pinkish-brown cap and bright yellow non-bruising pores; cap surface may taste sour.
mushroom_profile.season_note: June through September in oak/hardwood woods; MushroomExpert describes it as summer and fall, and Indiana collections include June.

records.mushroom_card_note: Eastern oak-forest bolete with sticky pinkish-brown cap and bright yellow non-bruising pores; cap surface may taste sour.
records.field_identification: Look for sticky pinkish-brown to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow pore surface that does not bruise, white basal mycelium, and a slender reddish/yellowish stem. Mycorrhizal with oaks and other hardwoods in eastern North America.
records.edibility_detail: Confirm sticky pinkish-brown cap, bright yellow non-bruising pores, white basal mycelium, and oak/hardwood association. Do not infer food value from yellow pores alone.

use_links.label: Wikimedia Commons: Aureoboletus auriporus

records.edibility_notes: Confirm sticky pinkish-brown cap, bright yellow non-bruising pores, white basal mycelium, and oak/hardwood association. Do not infer food value from yellow pores alone.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.
